ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for rice noodle set meals; FOODON_03315671 (noodle dish) is the closest parent class. Specific set-meal composite not represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers rice noodles as a food category; no composite set-meal code exists. Used as the primary ingredient anchor representing the dominant carbohydrate component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for rice noodle set meal composite dish.

serving.description: 1 set for 2 persons (estimated ~800g total edible weight, ~400g per person)
serving.natural_language_portion: One realistic serving for one person from a shared 2-pax set is approximately 400g, comprising half a bowl of rice noodles with beef topping, one drink, a portion of crispy rice cake, and pickled radish.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Rice Noodle Soup with Beef (composite reference); supplemented with HPB entries for rice noodle dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at 0.95 confidence for all per_100g macros and micronutrients. The composite set meal (braised beef rice noodle + brisket rice noodle + drinks + rice cake + pickled radish) does not appear as a single HPB entry; values are accepted from the input as HPB-sourced estimates for the blended dish composition. Saturated fat not reported in input; estimated at 1.4g/100g based on HPB beef noodle soup reference entries. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown and full micronutrient panel not available in HPB for this composite.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — GI value 61 (analogous estimate); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists for this specific composite set meal. GI of 61 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice noodles, which is the dominant carbohydrate component. The set includes side items (crispy rice cake, pickled radish, drinks) that may modestly raise the effective GI; 61 is therefore a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100g × serving_g / 100) / 100 = (61 × 25.19 × 400 / 100) / 100 = 61.5; reported as 15.4 per 100g-equivalent single-person portion basis for template consistency.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231mg/100g; shared set with broth-heavy dishes can accumulate 800–1200mg sodium per person per meal
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced broth and sauce across all dishes in the set, and avoid drinking the soup base, to keep total sodium intake within the 1500–2000mg/day DASH-diet target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles GI ~61 (medium); carbohydrate 25.19g/100g moderated by dietary fibre 3.7g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit their portion to half the set share (~200g noodle component), prioritise the protein topping over noodles, and avoid sweetened drinks included in the set to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Red meat (braised beef, beef brisket) contributes saturated fat and dietary cholesterol; sodium load from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit red meat portions, request lean cuts or substitute with chicken or tofu where available, reduce broth consumption to lower sodium and saturated fat intake, and ensure total saturated fat from this meal does not exceed 7% of daily energy.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef) high in arachidonic acid; broth sodium may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise the beef components of this set and request substitution with chicken, seafood, or tofu toppings; broth consumption should be limited to reduce sodium-driven tissue inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The 2-Pax Rice Noodle Set (双人分享米线套餐) is a Chinese-style shared meal designed for two diners, served at a Singapore restaurant specialising in Yunnan-style rice noodle dishes. The set comprises two bowls of rice noodles: one topped with spicy braised beef (红烧牛肉米线, Tang Xiaojie style) and one with non-spicy clear-broth beef brisket (清汤牛腩米线). Accompaniments include two beverages, a portion of crispy glutinous rice cake coated in red sugar (红糖糍粑), and pickled radish (爽口脆萝卜). Rice noodles (米线, mǐxiàn) are thin, smooth noodles made from rice flour, with a soft, slightly chewy texture and mild flavour that absorbs the savoury beef broth. The braised beef variant is richly spiced with chilli and aromatics, while the brisket variant is lighter and cleaner in flavour. Per 100g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, 25.19g carbohydrate, 3.7g dietary fibre, and 231mg sodium. The moderate fibre content partially offsets the medium glycemic index (~61) of rice noodles. Clinically, the sodium load from broth is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients; red meat content is a concern for rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. This dish is culturally associated with Yunnan province cuisine, popularised in Singapore through specialty rice noodle restaurants, and is typically consumed at lunch or dinner as a satisfying shared meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a 2-pax rice noodle set?
Is 双人分享米线套餐 suitable for diabetics?
Sodium content of beef rice noodle set Singapore
Is braised beef rice noodle good for hypertension?
Can I eat rice noodle set with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Input record carries HPB provenance at 0.95 confidence for all per_100g values; however, this is a composite set meal (two noodle bowls + sides + drinks) and no single HPB entry covers this exact combination. Values are accepted as HPB-sourced blended estimates. Saturated fat was estimated from HPB beef noodle reference entries. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice noodles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, full vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite set meal are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the input HPB record; confirmed zero per HPB input data, though the presence of beef in the dish makes this value uncertain — it may reflect a blended/averaged composite where cholesterol was not separately measured. 3. Saturated fat estimated at 1.4g/100g based on HPB beef noodle soup reference entries (adapted). 4. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate for boiled rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite set meal GI may be higher due to the crispy rice cake and sweetened drinks. 5. Serving size of 400g per person is an estimate for one person's share of the 2-pax set; actual portion weight may vary by restaurant. 6. Halal status not confirmed; the restaurant and dish have not been verified as MUIS-certified halal. 7. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact codes exist for this composite set meal.
